speaker: interviewer
text: This is take two, interview with Henry Moser. It's January 24, 1996. You were talking about seeing businesses that were boycotted.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of the businesses, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them. The-- the-- the cleaning supply store was called [NON-ENGLISH], Water Bird. But that was evidently the name of the people. I don't know. There was one chain store I remember. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were other Jewish chain stores. [? Winston ?] department stores. But after that initial, that boycott day, I think it was in April 1 or something, 1933-- I'm not sure exactly when it was. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Most of these major businesses, the Jewish people, left. Sold it, evidently. I really am ignorant about what they did. They were not boycotted, per se. And while there were incidents evidently all during those years, I haven't seen any in Berlin until November the-- the-- the 10, 1938, when they smashed the windows of-- a lot of Jewish business was single stores. But they did have chain stores. And I can't think of-- of a name of-- or any other, except the department stores. There was Tietz, Wertheim, and so on, which is still existing under that name. Karstadt, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the five and ten. There were several. [NON-ENGLISH] and so on. And they were-- they were not just in Germany. They were also in Switzerland. I don't know, even in other-- in other countries. I don't know. I know they were in Switzerland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you hear on the radio or see in the newspapers about what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I really don't remember too much about it. I know I did read-- read a little bit in the newspapers, I mean, little incidents and things like that when I-- when I was about 10 years old, 11 years old. I did read some of the news. I did read sometimes something in the newspaper. Radio, my parent's radio broke. And my father said, I don't want to hear the Nazi talk. And we didn't have a radio anymore after 1933 or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your parents tell you about the Nazis?

speaker: interviewee
text: But we did read paper. We did read the newspaper. We didn't read the Nazi paper, particularly Nazi papers. But the others had to print-- there was still other papers around. But they had to print basically the same news. The outspoken Nazi papers usually had big striking headlines, either anti-Semitic, anti-Roosevelt, or anti-American, or so on. The other papers didn't quite-- weren't quite that bad at the time. But that's what I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the names at the big Nazi papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: One was And then there was a one called that was run by somebody named Julius Streicher. That was a-- an obscene cartoons on the front. I remember the obscene anti-Semitic cartoons. That's all. He was an expert in only anti-Semitism. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's what I remember. I would never pick up that paper, of course. But I you can't help seeing it being sold.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of cartoons?

speaker: interviewee
text: What the what?

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of cartoons? What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't remember details. But-- but the basic anti-Semitic cartoons are pretty well-known. You still see them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see any parades in the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the street? Any-- any what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Parades.

speaker: interviewee
text: Parades, yes. Couldn't help seeing them, because our school was-- the Jewish school I went was right on the main-- main street where the Nazis always came through. It was a parade street. There was a street that finally by changing a few times names was-- was wound up under [NON-ENGLISH], which is pretty well-known, where all the embassies and-- and a lot of consulates for foreign countries were at the one end. And at the-- at the the other end, was-- was where our school was. Today, it's a television station in Berlin. The grounds were bought. This Jewish school was on the ground that was by one of the Jewish department stores owned it. They were going to put a department store there. With the upcoming of Nazis, they never did. And the-- the old building was remodeled by the school. And we-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I mean, they-- the property was sold to the radio station. The Berlin radio station was on the rear side of-- of the property across the street. There was a huge building which was the radio station. Served, of course, the propaganda, of the Nazi propaganda at that time. And they had bought the grounds. But they didn't kick us out immediately. They just took half the land away. There still was enough land for-- for us there, for the school grounds and everything. But they owned it. There was some little incidents there. For instance, a ball went over the fence. And one big fellow who went to my class, he went over there and asked for the ball. The Nazis said, we'll give you the ball back if you let us slap your face.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, go ahead. [LAUGHS] He was big kid. Well, this comes to mind since you asked me. Those little incidents. That was in the mid-- like, 1934, '35.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the-- and-- and, of course, on the other side, the front of the building faced-- what's it called-- what at that time was called [NON-ENGLISH]. It was-- it was that main street that wound up going through the Brandenburg Gate into [NON-ENGLISH]. It was all the same street. It had many different names and still does. But we were-- and at the end, there was a big square. They named it after Hitler at the time. It's still named after whoever is the chancellor in Germany. And several other streets-- it dissipated into several other streets from there on. But it was the street where all the parades where. It was also probably the widest street in Berlin. It was very, very wide.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the radio station?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the-- was the Berlin Radio Station. And-- and I think they also bought [NON-ENGLISH] which is  called the Deutschland Center. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And like I said, they still-- still, now on the spot where our school was, it's a-- it's a television station. The radio station is still there. So as far as the property, this was always owned by the government since that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your parents tell you about what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Enough, I guess, so I realized what was going on very much. When my father lost his job, I was 10-- 10 years old. And I did pretty well realize it. My father got a notice, six months notice.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he called up the president of the corporation, it was a corporation, who was a good friend of his. He said, that's ridiculous. I retract it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two days later, we get a-- we get a letter from-- you see any kind of-- of a-- especially newspapers, but it was in other factories and so on and other businesses, too, where there were more-- of-- more than eight employees, I remember that. The eight employees I remember. They had to have one man form the party in there to be the manager over and above the president of the corporation or chairman of the board. They had really nothing to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he got a letter from this man. His name was Gemer, I'll never forget, G-E-M-E-R, and a very nice letter, very polite letter. Not-- I mean, he said, sorry. But so and so-- and I forgot his name, but I have it some place on record here. Because my father had a-- had a-- had a-- letter of recommendation from him.

speaker: interviewee
text: That it means nothing-- he has nothing to say, he said. Under the law of the Third Reich, I have to-- I have to give you notice. And you have to-- we can't-- Jews are not allowed to be employed in any kind of a public paper, which this was. But he asked him in the same letter if he knows somebody that could take his place in Berlin. And he asked him in the same letter about one particular person, which my father did not know. And I don't have that letter anymore. I looked for it. I looked all day for it today. I looked for it before. Too bad. Even though it's in German, it's a classic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of the people in the streets bother you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not until the so-called Crystal Night. Not me, but others where evidently. It depends on what neighborhood you were, were bothered. We-- I went to ORT-- to the ORT trade school on the 10th of November innocently. On the 9th of November, the night, was when the burned the synagogues. My-- I had to take a train to go to the school. And I had to-- it was a city train. But it was a train. And I didn't know anything til I got to school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then other kids said, did you see synagogue? Is the synagogue burning? No, I didn't. I didn't go by there. And then they called us together in the school and told us we send you home in little groups, three and four, no more.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were worried. We went home. Of course, we were shocked. By that time, because I was-- I was 15 years old, we weren't kidding ourselves for anything anymore, about anything anymore. So we-- we went in little groups home. And in the train, people did realize we were Jewish. Some how you could realize it. And they looked away, obviously, like we didn't exist. Some people were ashamed. There's no doubt about it. The-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some of the Catholic hierarchy-- and I don't know names-- offered the Jews the churches for services, which Jews did not accept anyway. But I must say that they must have felt they may be next, some. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the requisition of Jewish property, what do you know about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what brought on the-- the Crystal Night was, if you remember one embassy employer, actually a [INAUDIBLE] of it in Switzerland, was killed by somebody named Grynszpan, who was Jewish. And-- and they wanted a reparation of 1 billion mark from the Jewish. And they took, basically they-- they-- they from those people that had bank accounts, I guess they took it. We didn't have it. We didn't have any. They couldn't take it from us. We had no [INAUDIBLE]. We had no money. But the 1 billion mark was a-- was a lot of money. And they took it from those Jews who had it in the banks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- that was what they called reparations for the murder. And from then on, things got bad. Like, in  a street car, if you were Jewish, and sat down, you may have been-- then you may have had incidents. It happened to me once that somebody said something. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember one case and somebody else defended me. And it was very bad. I got off as fast as I could. Somebody, not Jewish, defended me in that same streetcar. I haven't forgotten that.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I mean, I mean, that was the only time it may-- may-- happened to me directly. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Except from where we-- when we moved in a new neighborhood, the kids where somewhat-- somewhat anti-Semitic, you know. I just withdrew from them, and we moved out for a short time. But, otherwise, I cannot remember personally being any otherwise exposed to it. I was lucky I suppose. I was a little guy. I was lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: So do you recall them taking anything from your family or anything that you had to give them?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were lucky. They didn't come to arrest my father or me. But friends of ours they did, as a matter of fact, a number of them. The one man, he had a limp. I think it was from World War I, an injury. And they arrested right away at a little tobacco store.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one of the Gestapo men felt to ask him, is that a war injury you have? He said, yes. He said, oh, you can't march. And he sent him home. He was lucky.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he warned everybody. The best thing is don't be home for a while after that. That's how we heard about the arrests. That's the first thing I think we heard about the arrests, that way, from that man.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was around maybe the 11th of November or 11th or 12th of November, right after the Crystal Night.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was the person? Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. His name was Rabinowich. I forgot his first name. His son went to school with us. The son's name was Hans. [INAUDIBLE] They wanted to arrest the son, too. And then the wife said, if you come you ask only for my husband. She had enough guts that they did leave the son alone. He was only 16 or 15. He was only 15 1/2 maybe. That was-- yeah, that I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know about the Nuremberg Laws?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Nuremberg Laws?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that affect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I knew about it, because it was part of the reason my father lost his job. That-- this part, I knew about it. And I knew that they were dangerous to us. And I knew our lives were basically in danger. I knew that, too, since I was about 11 or 12. But we lived five floors up in the last apartment we lived. And every time somebody came up the stairs, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you were scared. No people came up those stairs, because we lived in a-- in a divided apartment. And that used to be the servant entrance where we lived at that time. We had to have a cheap place. And these apartments were big apartments before World War I. After World War I, they divided a lot of apartments. And not many people came up there. Not every apartment in that house was divided. The one up there was, so few people really walked up there. The mail man had to come up there. And people who read the gas meter and the power meter had to come up there. But outside this, if we-- unless we expected some visitors, practically nobody came up those stairs. The people below us, it wasn't divided. And it-- while it was a servant entrance, a lot of the people didn't have servants, didn't even use it anymore. They came the front, we have an elevator in front. And lower down, we wouldn't hear it anyway. So when we-- when we heard them coming up, they were already at the third or forth floor, and you were scared. You never knew when they'd come get you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to synagogue during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I went to synagogue. I-- I-- I got-- I got very friendly with a kid in my class in the Jewish school. And my parents got friendly with-- with their parents. And we-- and he was-- they were not Orthodox. But he became Orthodox. His grandmother was Orthodox. And he became-- he-- he went there. Usually, he picked me up, because it was on the way. I lived on the way to the-- to the nearest synagogue. And he picked me up Friday night and Saturdays. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So-- so I went pretty regularly.

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents not, but I did during those years til they burned down the synagogues. There's a plaque there, a plaque on the wall of [NON-ENGLISH], which is on that place. When I was back there, I seen the plaque. As far as I'm concerned, what for? What was the name of the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was [NON-ENGLISH] temple. Temple of peace, [NON-ENGLISH] in Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened after Crystal Night? Tell me how your life changed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we desperately tried to find ways to leave Germany. There was no help from Jewish organization. You were over 35, and you had no trade. And the answer was, of the people who worked there, they were themselves-- I can't help myself. I can't help you. They were of help to younger people. They got a lot of young people that got arrested and thrown in concentration camp out. And then came the story about Shanghai, that Shanghai, was-- it was possible to go to Shanghai without a visa as long as you could pay your trip.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a passport?

speaker: interviewee
text: We applied for it after we had the tickets. We had to buy the tickets. Others could buy the-- if you had an open bank account, you could buy the tickets for the-- most ships that were going were Italian ships, some Japanese ships. We had to-- we had-- the money came, because my inheritance- the house was being sold for very little money. There wasn't any money left for us. We already had lived on that money before ahead of time. But my uncle had-- had-- had lived in France. He had an account. It was Devisen account, they called it. This was an account which was open for him. But he could only pay bills with it. He could not take the money out of Germany. He couldn't take it out in Germany except for paying bills. So we-- if we bought the bill for the shipping line, the account was allowed to pay them. So we had to take a German ship. And the-- and that was in December 1938. And-- the-- the-- the closest ship we could get was supposed to leave on June 10. And then that was full, too. And it became the July 10. They left-- there were three ships. They left every month on the same day. And we made it out on July 10. And we applied for the passport. And it came about around the middle of early June. We still didn't have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father went to police headquarters. You had to apply through the police. Really, he had guts. And we were lucky. He went to [INAUDIBLE]. And there was an old man there, a veteran from World War I.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very many police people, the old people were veterans from World War I. They gave them a police job. When they got old, they give them a desk job. He had a desk job. And he said, oh, yeah, you're going to leave  in four or five weeks. You better get your passport. I am going to get it for you. And he did get our three passports. We were lucky that this man was decent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was--

speaker: interviewee
text: It could have been-- gotten a-- gotten a young guy, who may have thrown us all in concentration camp for having the-- having the-- the audacity to  ask for a passport. My father, actually I think it was foolish in a way. We may have gotten it. We may have not gotten it in time. But that was no time-- I mean, if you-- if you get to the wrong person, it was it. So we were lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you by chance know this person's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't. I don't-- not-- there is a name on the passport. I don't think it may  not necessarily be his name. He just was good. I'm going to get it for you. And he got it for us in a very short time, a half an hour or so or less. And-- He was decent, obviously.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your passport like? How did it identify you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, look, they put the name Israel in for every man and Sarah for every Jewish woman. Sarah being not a-- a common name in the-- in the-- not in the gentile world. Like, for instance, in America or even in England, it's a very common name. But in Germany, it was not. It was strictly a Jewish name.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you hear about Shanghai? Who told you about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents heard it. I-- I don't remember. But they had some friends, some people. They went to school to learn how to cut shirts and make shirts. I think I told you that they tried to learn a trade. And they-- they knew. They went before we went. Their name was [? Levy ?]. I remember that. I didn't know them. I met them later in Shanghai. But they-- they definitely told them. Now, maybe my-- my-- parents heard it from someplace else, too. I don't know. My father was still working for the Jewish community there. And maybe he heard it there, that you could go. I really don't remember that part. But he got the tickets. And we got out. We were lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me about the ship? And what did you take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were able to take with us a lot of things we had. Like, we had some crystal and stuff like that. Mother had a sewing machine, a typewriter. But they were not new. If you take it new, they allowed it. They allowed it out. But you had to pay 100% custom or whatever you call it to take it out. We just didn't have that money.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got some money from that account of my uncle to get us decent clothings and so on, for-- for which it had to-- it lasted us eight years, OK? And things like that, yes. But it wasn't-- it wasn't unlimited either, that account. There wasn't that much there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had to pay round trip. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We didn't know why. But there's a book by David Kranzler, which is probably the best book writing about the Jews in Shanghai. I don't know if you want to take a picture of it later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, we can take a look at it.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's an excellent, almost an historical book rather than a story. And-- I-- and-- and the man did fantastic work on that book. And he probably much more objective than any of us who were there. Because when you're in the middle of something, you're not as objective as when you do research like he did and got all kinds of information, and then got it pretty much the nail right on the head with whatever he says in that book. I have to-- nothing in for me, believe me. But it is a good-- it is a good book to look it up. And he says in that book, why. The answer I found in that book. I never knew why. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because the Jewish refugees came to Shanghai from the end of '38 already. There were a few that were there for years before-- I'm not talking about them-- from Germany and Austria. Did any kind of work, including manual work, which was face losing for the Caucasians in Shanghai if you did work like the Chinese did, like the Chinese laborers or [INAUDIBLE] or craftsmen. And so they tried to stop that immigration. The harbor of Shanghai was controlled by the Japanese who had in '37 taken over the whole Northern China practically in the war with China. Southern China, they never got. But Northern China, they did. Shanghai, they-- they-- they took part of  an international settlement, mainly where a lot of Japanese lived, civilians from before I believe, and annexed that to their area. And on the other side of the river still was international settlement and the French concession. The French concession was like a French colony. The international settlement was run by a municipal government of nine people which were, they have to be, by their constitution or what, five British, two Americans, and two Japanese. And we lived in the part we called [INAUDIBLE], which was taken over when the Japanese was bombed.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to--

speaker: interviewee
text: And they wanted the Japanese to stop us to get through. Because there was about a 30 [INAUDIBLE], about a 30 mile river travel from-- from the-- from the sea through the Yangtze mouth to Shanghai to the [INAUDIBLE] river. And on the way there, the Japanese custom [INAUDIBLE] came aboard. And they could have stopped us, but did not. And they wanted them to stop it. And the Japanese took their good time. They didn't really want to do it at that time. And they consulted a couple of very influential Jews-- and it is in the book there-- if it was all right. And they figured it was quite all right. And that happened in May 1939, before we-- before we-- we got on the ship. We did not know this that they were [INAUDIBLE]. And the Japanese dragged it out, I think, till October, till they finally did close-- close it without money. You had to have $400 American or something, which was a-- a tremendous sum. And they allowed us to take out $4 American, which was 10 marks. And so this happened in May. Now, the shipping companies asked-- they-- they knew that for long before that there was move on the way to try to stop us from landing. This is why they in December already 1938 wanted a round-trip ticket paid in case they had to take us back. But we luckily didn't know all of that. It would've driven us farther up the wall. We got there in August, 10th of August.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me again what date you left.

speaker: interviewee
text: 10th of July.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anybody else that you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: On that ship, that I'm-- no. But we met, there were about 150 refugees on the ship, partially in first class, partially in tourist class. We were in tour-- whatever there was available. And there were about another 200 other passengers in that ship. And we were allowed on the ship to have 400 marks. We didn't even have that much. Per person, ship money, that you were allowed to have. And we traded the ship money with other passengers for English pounds or American dollars. So they put up a notice that on a German [INAUDIBLE], the change of foreign currency is prohibited.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people made a joke says, well, we're not doing anything [INAUDIBLE]. We hold it over the ocean. People did it. And, you know, who did the-- who did-- who did the-- strangely enough, who did the most stealing and gave the best exchange rate were German business people on the ship. Ironic. There  were a few of them that did that. Not all of them, of course not.

speaker: interviewee
text: We found out something else which nobody seemed to have known in Europe. I knew from since my uncle was in France, you could buy the German mark in Europe for very little even in 1939. As soon as we hit Singapore, we found out that the German money was just as good as English pound or the American dollar.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all the German business people had to do is take-- buy some money cheap in-- in, for instance, in France, go to Asia, and sell it. That's all they had really to do to become wealthy. So some of them-- that's my guess. Some of them probably did, because they were not dumb. And they were usually traveling business people or salespeople or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a good time to change tapes.
